_note: Gap-fill for the analyst's wearers-to-customers ratio question. Grounds the customer-base size mix and the multi-service (non-uniform) revenue mix that explain why uniform wearers per customer is structurally low. The deck's <8M wearers / <2M customers figures are image-PDF / management framing and are NOT grounded here.

proposals.statement: Cintas serves over one million businesses of all types, from small service and manufacturing companies to major corporations employing thousands of people — i.e., a customer base spanning the full size range but dominated by small businesses by count.
proposals.quote: We provide our products and services to over one million businesses of all types, from small service and manufacturing companies to major corporations that employ thousands of people.

proposals.statement: Approximately 95% of Cintas's revenue is from route servicing across Uniform Rental & Facility Services, First Aid & Safety Services and Fire Protection Services — so a large share of customers buy facility, first-aid or fire offerings, not necessarily rented uniforms.
proposals.quote: Approximately 95% of the Company's revenue is derived from fees for route servicing of Uniform Rental and Facility Services, First Aid and Safety Services and Fire Protection Services customers, performed by a Cintas employee-partner, at the customer's location of business.

proposals.statement: UniFirst likewise serves businesses of all sizes across multiple industries and sectors, confirming a broad, size-diverse customer base rather than a concentration of large accounts.
proposals.quote: We serve businesses of all sizes across multiple industries and sectors.

proposals.statement: Because the combined base spans over a million customers dominated by small businesses and a large share buy facility-services / first-aid / fire offerings rather than (or alongside) rented uniforms, the average number of uniform wearers per customer is structurally low — the base is broad and shallow, not a few large uniformed accounts.
